              U.S. DEPARTMENT OF LABOR
                   Ray Marshall, Secretary

                  BUREAU OF LABOR STATISTICS
                Janet L. Norwood, Acting Commissioner

               Employment and Earnings is prepared by the
           Division of Industry Employment Statistics and the
           Division of Employment and Unemployment Anal-
           ysis in collaboration with the Division of Special
           Publications. The data are collected by the Bureau
           of the Census (Department of Commerce), State
           Employment Security Agencies, and State Depart-
           ments of Labor in cooperation with the Bureau of
           Labor Statistics. A brief description of the co-
           operative statistical programs of the BLS with
           these agencies is presented in the Explanatory
           Notes. The State agencies are listed on the inside
           back cover.

                   Employment and Unemployment
                   Developments, October 1978

                   down slightly. The Nation's overall unemployment rate was        percent in October, unchanged from September and about
                   5.8 percent, compared with 6.0 percent in September and          a full percentage point above October 1977.

                                                                        Explanatory Notes
                                    These explanatory notes provide information on the concepts, methodology, and scope of Household Data
                                    (A tables), Establishment Data (B, C, and D tables), and State and Area Unemployment Data (E table) pub-
                                    lished in Employment and Earnings.



                                                                                Introduction

                      The statistics in this periodical are compiled from two major            Employment
                   sources: (1) Household interviews, and (2) reports from employers.

                       There are two types of errors possible in an estimate based on a    in the CPS.
                   sample survey—sampling and nonsampling. The standard errors                 Sampling error. Since the estimates are based on a sample, they
                   provided primarily indicate the magnitude of the sampling error.        may differ somewhat from the figures that would have been ob-
                   They also partially measure the effect of some nonsampling errors       tained if a complete census had been taken using the same schedules,
                   in response and enumeration but do not measure any systematic           instructions, and enumerators. The standard error is primarily a
                   biases in the data.                                                     measure of sampling variability, that is, of the variation that
                       Nonsampling errors. The full extent of nonsampling error is         occurs by chance because a sample rather than the entire popula-
                   unknown, but special studies have been conducted to qualify some        tion is surveyed. The chances are about 68 out of 100 that an
                   sources of nonsampling error in the CPS as discussed below. The         estimate from the survey differs from a complete census figure
                   effect of nonsampling error should be small on estimates of relative    by less than the standard error. The chances are about 95 out of
                   change, such as month-to-month change. Estimates of monthly             100 that the differences would be less than twice the standard
                   levels would be more severely affected by the nonsampling error.        error.

                   Labor turnover                                                         ployees are excluded from the employment estimates if the work
                      Labor turnover is the gross movement of wage and salary work-       stoppage extends through the report period.
                  ers into and out of employed status with respect to individual
                  establishments. This movement, which relates to a calendar month,
                  is divided into two broad types: Accessions (new hires and rehires)     ESTIMATING METHODS
                  and separations (terminations of employment initiated by either
                  employer or employee). Each type of action is cumulated for a               The principal features of the procedure used to estimate em-
                  calendar month and expressed as a rate per 100 employees. The           ployment for the industry statistics are (1) the use of the "link
                  date relate to all employees, whether full- or part-time, permanent     relative" technique, which is a form of ratio estimation, (2) periodic
                  or temporary, including executive, office, sales, other salaried        adjustment of employment levels to new benchmarks, and (3) the
                  personnel, and production workers. Transfers to another establish-      use of size and regional stratification.
                  ment of the company are included, beginning with January 1959.
                      Accessions are the total number of permanent and temporary
                  additions to the employment roll, including both new and rehired
                  employees.                                                              The "link relative" technique
                                                                                              From a sample composed of establishments reporting for both
                      New hires are temporary or permanent additions to the em-           the previous and current months, the ratio of current month em-
                   ployment roll of persons who have never before been employed in        ployment to that of the previous month is computed. This is
                   the establishment (except employees transferring from another          called a "link relative." The estimates of employment (all employees,
                   establishment of the same company) or of former employees              including production and nonproduction workers together) for
                   not recalled by the employer.                                          the current month are obtained by multiplying the estimates for
                                                                                          the previous month by these "link relatives." In addition, small

                   Table I. Summary of methods for computing industry statistics on employment, hours, earnings, and labor turnover

                                                                        Seasonal adjustment




                      Many economic statistics reflect a regularly recurring seasonal         The seasonal adjustment programs used for these series are an
